transcript:
What you're watching start as scattered pink dashes on near-black is 800 particles slowly stitching themselves into fur-like currents — and by the end, the whole canvas is breathing as one surface.

Here's the thing hiding in plain sight: the color you see isn't random, it's a running tally. Look at "drawTrail" — each particle's hue is locked to its "encounters" count, so red means barely-touched, blue means moderately social, gold and eventually "memoryPurple" mean it's been jostled by neighbors dozens of times. The pink-to-purple gradient you see isn't decoration, it's literally a visible history of how crowded each particle's life has been.

And the fading itself is deliberate — in the draw loop, the comment calls it "the palimpsest," where old decisions ghost beneath new ones, at just 4% opacity per frame. That's why nothing ever fully vanishes; every past position stays faintly legible under the current one.

So what looks like fur or fabric is actually a memory structure — encounter counts stacked as color, decay rate stacked as depth. The texture you're seeing is statistics, rendered as skin.
